speaker: interviewer
text: --in the ghetto. Or you're about to be assembled for the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'll talk about first they assembled us to the synagogue, where we very docilely went. Took whatever we could and left the doors open. House, that's it. And we were there quiet because after all, the Germans don't want enemy people to be next to the-- to the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] war zone. And then they took us on horse-drawn, drawn wagons to the town of [NON-ENGLISH] where they set up a ghetto for all of these surrounding towns and villages. And here again, it's only retrospect that I realize things. It didn't go by whether that town was bigger or smaller, because there was another ghetto in a-- in a village, which was smaller than my town. It only mattered that it was near the railroad tracks. And our town was at least seven miles away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they wanted us assembled near the trains, near the railroads. So they assembled us in this ghetto where they took big houses of Jews and put in like six, seven families in one room. The rooms, by the way, in Europe were much bigger than here. So one big room could-- you know, to just lie down could house several families, and you could walk around, too, on the floor, of course. And that's where we were. How did they allocate food for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know, you know? They didn't give us any food-- whatever we brought, I imagine. I don't know. Either I was too young or I was too stupid to really look into all this. I don't know. But we had food. My mother prepared food. It wasn't as good as at home and all that, but there we were. I didn't-- and of course, I met all kinds of young people there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the work allocated? You know, keeping the place neat or clean or washing.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no allocation, really. Everyone did it just on their own, just what, what would your neighbor say, I imagine, sort of thing. But we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for money and buying things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever you brought. I don't know. I don't know. I didn't think we had that much money, but somehow we did not go hungry. And we were there very, nicely docilely. Nobody ran away. Nobody did anything. At one point we heard that some young people wanted to run away. It was possible. It was definitely possible, but the parents usually didn't let them. We want to be together, together, together all the time. My brother certainly could-- would have had no problem whatsoever. And then a neighbor from a hometown came there and begged my parents to give them the younger children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother's reaction was with goyim? I mean, how could we? And all this is because of ignorance. Had we known where we are going, you'd put them with goyim and anybody just to save them.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, after the war, I had-- used to have nightmares or imaginings. I, I imagined that I took the younger children, and I built them into a wall. And then you could get in there through the basement, and somebody would come and feed them. And then after the war I came to get them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wished--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] I came to get those children so many times in my imagination. The children were so important to me. They weren't just siblings. They were my-- like my children. They were not just siblings. I took care of them. They loved me because I was always nice to them. My sister didn't have patience with them. My younger sister, she was 12 years old and she began hearing all kinds of things about menstruation. I myself didn't know about, and she would consult me, and they looked up to me. So after the war, I always imagined they were somewhere there and I'll go and save them. And I'll go and get him back. I'm not going to leave them there. But they were not anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course I knew it. I just imagined it. Had wished it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wished it because, as I said, those children were the age of my grandchildren now. And they were like my children. Whenever I got anything, I gave it to them. I got a piece of cloth for something. It wasn't big enough for me-- of course, the children, the children. Everything was for the children. And my neighbors came to ask for them-- for these children.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Vysk--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They came to ask for them. My mother would not let them go. And this is-- this is something to the credit-- what credit? The Germans that they kept us ignorant, and we didn't want to do any of that. And the older people could have run away. There was no problem about that, but my mother wanted us to be together, together, always together. Anyway, we were there for a couple weeks, three weeks, and then we were transferred to another area because maybe there were too many people all ready, because they were bringing others all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did the Germans demand of you while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, just to be quiet and docile. And we were quiet and docile. They-- in the morning, just to harass us a little, they took us out for, for exercises, for gymnastics because Jews are lazy and they don't do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what they explicitly said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You-- Jews are lazy. Yes, of course. And then they took some girls to their rooms to clean for them. I wasn't one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were very nice. I mean, what is there-- why are they complaining about the Germans? They were just so nice. They were probably given orders to be like that, and they really tried very hard. Well-- These weren't SS men?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the difference. I would guess they were. I would guess they were. And by the way, I read enough to know that it didn't really matter if it was SS or not. The others helped along too. So the, the German Wehrmacht, those, you know, in war you're supposed to be all kinds of things, but they helped SS. I would guess they were SS. And after seven weeks, about three days before, before Shavous, they loaded us on to cattle cars. Whatever you could carry, you could take.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave you those orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they are going to resettle us in the east so we can work there, and we believed it. And we believed that they were not really so bad. Nicely-- go, go on, go on. Not nicely, but not as bad as we might have expected. And we got onto those cattle cars. You could-- couldn't really sit down. Everybody couldn't sit at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there with your grandparents and your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they went to a different ghetto. I said that there were different ghettos at different places at the railroad, and they lived next to a village where there was a railroad. It didn't matter how the village was. The main thing was there was a-- they planned to take us further. This was just a gathering place-- a very temporary one at that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were put-- oh, we were examined for gold and money and silver. How did they examine you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Examined internally even. And I remember my mother begged them and says these girls-- you know, my sister and I were older-- they are not married. Please don't examine them internally. They did. And this was the population. I mean, it wasn't Germans who did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Women from, from the population of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or Ukrainians, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they invaded your bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And really tried to take away as much as possible. Then the rest they let us go on to cattle cars, and it was getting very hot. And there was not a window, and there wasn't food. Food, you were supposed to bring along, but what about drinks? We begged for drinks. And in the cattle cars I remember my father to whom everybody looked up. I mean, after all, he was an important, learned man. He says, nothing to worry about. Nothing to worry about because we are living in the 20th century. I mean, after all, and the Germans are a cultured nation. That's what he said. And then after a while he'd say, and besides, the world is looking on. They couldn't do anything bad. I mean, the world would know. The, the-- they would prevent it. All these things which sound so stupid now, which sounds so ridiculous now, but this is what he said. And then he started looking at the stations. There was a little air hole so we could look out. He looked at the stations. He knew-- he knew geography. And he figured out that we are going towards Poland from the names of, of the stations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says well, Poland is not a very good place, but they have to take us away from, from the front. He wasn't worried-- absolutely not worried. There were some old women-- nobody in our car died, but some of the others people died. Children couldn't-- they screamed and there was no toilet. So there were pots being used. It was terrible and hot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just you couldn't even sit down. You had to take turns sitting down. Finally, finally after three days, we arrived to a town-- [NON-ENGLISH], Auschwitz. We knew nothing, absolutely nothing. It meant nothing to us. And some people who looked like from an insane asylum got onto the cars-- out, out, out, and start pushing. Take everything. Leave everything here. You'll get it back. Out, out, out. We found out afterwards they were Jewish prisoners. Well, they looked like people from an insane asylum with striped pajamas. We didn't know what was happening, and they gave us all kinds of hints, which we didn't understand. We didn't know. He says don't, don't say that you have a twin. Don't say. And give, give the children to-- give babies to the grandmother. And don't carry a baby because there was women, 22, 23 carrying a baby. And they knew that carrying a baby will mean straight to the gas chambers. So and look healthy, and walk erect. So we asked why. Quiet, go, go, go and so on. So we didn't really know what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: They told us all these things, but only afterwards did it make sense. At that time, it didn't make sense. We thought these people are crazy anyway, so how can you listen to them? So we were brought down from the cars and there was this Mengele man, tall with white gloves with a-- with, with-- it wasn't a sword. It was a-- what do you call it? A stick and some dogs. And it was getting to be evening, and you had to walk by. And he says right, left, right, left-- just like that. And we didn't know what it meant, but we began wondering what it meant. They were work camps, and there were camps where women and children go. Right? Camps. Because they need work.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, after all, there are many healthy people here. They told us we were going to work camp. Because my little sister, had we known, she was getting older. She could have been standing up straight and walk with my big sister and me, and maybe gotten saved. [SOBBING] My mother was 45 years old. She was healthy, and beautiful. I'm now older than my grandmother was. We didn't even think of her. She had to take care of the children, so she certainly couldn't go with us. But there was absolutely no reason for her not to come with us. She was a healthy woman. She  could have done anything, but she had to go and take care of the children. And my little sister-- I have a picture of-- she also could have gone with my big sister and me, but not knowing-- ignorance, ignorance, ignorance. That's all it took is ignorance, and with-- and a-- and a vicious, evil person to make these plans, to make it work. We didn't know. So people, sometimes, they're like sheep. They went like sheep. Had they tried it, they would gave gone like sheep too. And besides, I don't have enough time for that, but I certainly have enough proof of that. So we were separated-- And we assumed my, my mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Men and women were separated. So my brother went with my father. My father was 52-years-old and very tall. You remember him? By the time you remember him, he was-- you know, he was older. But he was tall and walked erect. And he went with my brother, and my little brother who was also growing up, but no, he didn't survive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So we were marched into a shower room because Jews are not only lazy, they're also filthy as far as the Germans are concerned. So we had to be sent for showers. They told us to take everything off and just save our shoes and, and glasses-- anybody who had glasses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first thing they did was shave our heads because we must of full of lice, of course. I never saw a louse in my life before that. So they took all the hair afterwards, of course, you know and I know what happened to the hair. They used it for, for mattresses and all kinds of other things. They needed like-- like sheep are being shorn of their-- of the wool, we were shorn of our hair. And then we went to a shower, and we didn't know that the others don't have a shower. We assumed they had the same thing. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: The others meaning your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and the children, and anyone else they didn't like, or young women. By the way, this is again where they used their psychology. They didn't tear babies out of their young mothers arms, not at all. Why do we need a rebellion here? Mothers will start crying, won't they? Much easier to gas the mother also with the child, much easier. They just-- I mean, you go and take care of your child, of course. Here again, we just simply didn't know and did not believe these people came up to, to get us off the wagons because they did say that-- that we should not keep the children in our arms. But of course, many mothers I'm sure would have gone anyway just to be with their child, not knowing where they're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were showered and the shoes we had, the same shoes-- and here again, shoes you cannot start trying on. You cannot walk with shoes which are too small or too big. So isn't it much easier to let you use your-- keep your shoes? With dresses they didn't have to do that, because I got a dress which was up to the floor wide for an old woman. So what, does that make a difference? No. There was an older woman who got a cute little dress, short-sleeve, who was an older woman-- not old, no one was old, but an older woman who was much bigger and heavier and so on, so we exchanged. With shoes, you cannot do that. This was after the shower?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the shower. We exchanged, so I had a very nice dress, but no panties, no slip, nothing else-- a dress and your own shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So afterwards, people exchanged. People did all kinds of things because even there we didn't want to-- for one thing, I couldn't have walked in that dress, and she couldn't have put it on. So afterwards they were marching us towards what turned out to be C Lager in Birkenau. And as we were walking-- first of all, this 'arbei macht frei,' they accomplished a lot by that and the music. What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans-- you know they-- you have those-- they have the entrance of Auschwitz says, 'Arbei Macht Frei,' which means work frees you-- makes you free. Well, it made us feel like we're going to work, right? And music-- they're not going to start bothering with music if they mean to kill you. So all these things they accomplished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the music?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the entrance there was an orchestra playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean as you walked by and were chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the entrance. So this too, they accomplished what they were after. Mothers could go with their children, so they killed the mother too. Didn't matter. It's much more trouble to, to talk a mother into giving up a child. And we walked to the music, so they're probably not going to kill us then. Anyway, so we got into big barracks where there were three layers of, of bunk beds, if you can call them. They were huge. Three layers and on each layer, 12 to 14 people, and they weren't strong enough, so the upper layer fell down on the rest. It was-- it was pandemonium. It was awful. So still, it's working, we figured. And there were about 1,000 people in each of the barracks. There were about 22 barracks at the camp. There was one barrack all toilets, and one barrack all faucets with water for washing. And the rest were people. Now, each barrack had what they called a Blockalteste, which was the head of the block. And they were-- many of them were vicious, vicious Jews. They were in concentration camp for three years already. We thought they were like animals at that point. Really, they were just impossible. Some of them were nice enough, and others were simply furious with us. They hit us, saying, if you're so stupid to arrive here, you don't deserve to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said, why did you come here? Why were you so stupid? Didn't you hear what was happening? So they were simply furious. And we were stupid. So we didn't get any food till in the morning, we got a piece of bread, which looked like a piece of brick. And no one wanted to eat it. We weren't hungry enough yet. Also, I think there is a psychological aspect to it where we were-- our identity was torn away from us. We had no identity-- not our clothes, no hair, nothing. Who will know who I am? Who will know where I come from, who I am? So some people-- and that's not just a few-- many people in order to show how important they were from home and how they are not used to this kind of treatment refused to eat the bread because that showed that they're not used to bread like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they just refused to eat the bread because I'm not used to this sort of thing, which was simply to try and to take on some kind of an identity, which was taken away. I don't know why and how, but for some reason I refused to do that. I simply refused to do that. I said, I don't care what people think. This is the bread I have and this is the only thing I, I have to eat. And I will die if I don't eat it. I ate it. And of course, people thought well, this is what she's used to. I mean, after all it shows where she comes from. Well, I simply didn't care. I simply did not care. I figured there are other ways of, of taking on an identity, not by killing yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they gave some soup-- and this too afterwards I realized-- soup where I come from meant nice very watery soup. You know, because you had a meal afterwards. This was just like chicken soup, right? That doesn't have anything in it. You were smart enough. You put all kinds of other things in it, but no, of course not. So this had all kinds of things in it. And who wants soup look like this? We're not used to soup like this. I ate it. I ate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were just wandering around, and every morning, sel appel at 5 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the word?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sel appel, which was counting time-- you know, the time for counting. Stand in line, five to, to a line being counted-- counted again, counted again. And the weather there is absolutely the most horrible weather ever anyplace-- rainy and muddy and cold. May, this was May already. Very cold, and we of course were cold anyway because we only had a dress, not anything to cover ourselves, not anything to put on the-- above the planks of the beds. So we were always cold and standing in the rain and not food and nothing. And of course, if you didn't stay in the line, then somebody came with a whip and whipped you, and just like a nightmare-- just an absolute nightmare. But this was our life at that point. And we didn't have any idea where my parents were. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with someone you knew? Just my sister. And somehow we got into a barrack where no one from our town was there. And this went on. And there was no work. It's just that some people went to-- and I was once or twice one of them-- went to the rail, rails to bring some pots because we ate from pots. There were no dishes or plates or anything. So they'd give you some soup in a big pot for each row.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you drink it, I mean, 14 people from the same pot. And of course, you'd have to be sure you don't drink too much. And then, of course, my-- of course. I don't know why it's for of course, but my sister had a lot of trouble with me because I was very finicky and refused to drink if somebody drank first. Well, that was the place to be that way. Well my sister, believe it or not, worked very hard to see to it that I drank first. She took care of my craziness, really, and I still can't drink from anybody. So it was very, very, very hard. And as far as going to bring the pot I once or twice did on, on wagons-- a human-drawn wagon instead of horse drawn wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: You volunteered for this job? Yeah. They wanted someone. I went to be out of that camp.
